content.full_text:
State of California

Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814
Technical Assistance Administration       Executive Legal       Enforcement       Conflict of Interest
(916) 322 5660               322 5901             322 6441             322 6444

October 24, 1977


Justice Cruz Reynoso
Court of Appeal
Third Appellate District
Library and Courts Building
Sacramento, CA  95814

Dear Justice Reynoso:

     In response to your telephone inquiry, we advised you
that you are required to report on your Statement of Economic
Interests the receipt by your wife of food and accommodations
at a workshop sponsored by the California Academy of Appellate
Lawyers in June, 1976.  Recently, the staff has had occasion
to reassess its interpretation of the reporting requirements
which arise when an official's spouse is the receipient of a
gift.  The question will be submitted to the Commission at its
next meeting in November, and it may be that the final resolu-
tion of the matter will not to require reporting of benefits
of the type received by your wife at the Academy workshop.

     Since you originally inquired about this problem, we
wanted to advise you of this development and welcome any com-
ments you may have prior to the Commission's review of this
question.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]
                                        Michael J. Baker
                                        Chief
                                        Legal Division

MJB:jo

December 15, 1977

Honorable Cruz Reynoso
Associate Justice
Court of Appeal
Third Appellate District
State Library and Courts Building
Sacramento, CA 95814

Dear Justice Reynoso:

At its December 7, 1977, meeting the Fair Political
Practices Commission considered the question of whether
gifts of food, travel and lodging made to the spouse of an
official in connection with the official's participation in
a seminar or panel are reportable on the official's Statement
of Economic Interests. The Commission declared that such
gifts to the official's spouse are not reportable by the
official.

As I believe you are aware, Commission regulations
(2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18728) exempt from reporting food,
travel and lodging provided to an official in connection
with his or her participation in or speaking at an in-state
event, seminar or panel. The regulation does not exempt
from reporting food, travel and lodging provided to an of-
ficial's spouse who accompanied his or her to such an event.
However, based upon the Cory opinion (Opinion Requested by
Ken Cory, 2 PPC Opinions 48, No. 75-094-A, April 22, 1976)
which generally exempts from reporting gifts made to an offi-
cial's spouse and the policy, embodied in Section 18723,
that an official is unlikely to be improperly influenced by
food, travel and lodging provided in connection with panels
and seminars in which they participate, the Commission decided
that the provision of food, travel and lodging to an accom-
panying spouse should not be reportable.

The Commission's decision is contrary to previous
advice the Commission staff has provided to you. It is my
understanding that you have amended your Statement of Economic
Interests to include gifts to your spouse which under the

A-77-389

able Cruz Reynoso
mber 15, 1977
ge Two


Commission's interpretation are not reportable. Therefore,
you may, if you desire, amend your statements to delete the
reporting of the gifts to your wife.

We apologize for the inconvenience we have caused
you by altering our advice and thank you for your interest
which brought a difficult issue to our attention. If you
have any further questions, please do not hesitate to call
me.

Sincerely,


Michael J. Baker
Chief
Legal Division

MJB:plh

sections.conclusion: At its December 7, 1977, meeting the Fair Political Practices Commission considered the question of whether gifts of food, travel and lodging made to the spouse of an official in connection with the official's participation in a seminar or panel are reportable on the official's Statement of Economic Interests. The Commission decided that such gifts to the official's spouse are not reportable by the official.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether gifts of food, travel, and lodging provided to the spouse of an official in connection with the official's participation in seminars or panels must be reported on the Statement of Economic Interests.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC concluded that gifts of food, travel, and lodging provided to an official's spouse in connection with the official's participation in seminars or panels are not reportable on the Statement of Economic Interests, based on the Cory opinion and policy considerations that such benefits are unlikely to improperly influence the official.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains two letters dated October 24, 1977 and December 15, 1977. No formal 'QUESTION' or 'CONCLUSION' section headers present, but the second letter clearly articulates the question and the Commission's decision. This is a correction letter reversing prior staff advice, with an apology for the inconvenience caused.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Whether gifts of food, travel, and lodging provided to the spouse of an official in connection with the official's participation in seminars or panels must be reported on the Statement of Economic Interests.

CONCLUSION: At its December 7, 1977, meeting the Fair Political Practices Commission considered the question of whether gifts of food, travel and lodging made to the spouse of an official in connection with the official's participation in a seminar or panel are reportable on the official's Statement of Economic Interests. The Commission decided that such gifts to the official's spouse are not reportable by the official.

embedding.summary: This correspondence consists of two letters from FPPC Chief Michael J. Baker to Justice Cruz Reynoso regarding the reportability of gifts to an official's spouse. The FPPC initially advised reporting was required but later reversed course, determining that such gifts are not reportable.
